item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just have to agree with the people above and downvote because you have it outright neutralized.

You have to remember, and this is probably the ten-thousandth time that someone has said this, that this is the SCP Foundation, not the DDD Foundation. You should only have anomalies neutralized if they pose an actual threat, and stealing satellites to replicate itself isn't that dangerous. Maybe when it grabbed the missiles it would have become more dangerous and would have merited destruction, but as Kuraden said, nuking it would pose the risk that it takes that too.

I get that you wanted to link it in with this historical event; maybe to have had it merit destruction, it could have started… attacking us? Sending things down? As I said earlier, self-replicating ≠ OOOHHHEEEMMMGGGGEEE KETER NUKE IT (exaggeration).

If this feedback is taken into account, you will get a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher A: "Oh man, we tried to shoot it down and they grabbed the missile, took it apart, and incorporated the potentially destructive components into themselves! What the hell do we do now!?"

Researcher B: "Okay… hear me out… we shoot a nuke at it."

Researcher A: "Brilliant! there's no WAY they'll do the same thing even though we have no idea exactly what these things are capable of or if the feed on radiation and exothermic reactions. This couldn't POSSIBLY catastrophically backfire right in our face and give them nuclear weaponry!"

Who the hell authorized this and are they fired yet?
The risk of these space tribbles grabbing the nuke and using it on us far outweighs the potential threat of satellite-nappers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What is the deal with the five boxes at the bottom of the article? they do nothing.

no vote, it needs more substance

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I am quite curious what the transmission said. Otherwise not bad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is basically how I'm feeling; yes they're pretty damn sure that they wiped out the technorganic satellite and the things it was infecting. The thing that MADE it technorganic in the first place, the ET whatsit… Who's to say that was the only one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
despite the foundation's normal aversion to destroying SCP objects, the reaction here did seem appropriate to me, given the lack of control they had in the situation. i like it overall, upvoted.

however, i think this entry would benefit from some level of doubt being given as to the complete elimination of the threat. some note of concern as to the object's point of origin perhaps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is kind of unfair, but I also can't unthink it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that and I imagined the black thing being the Venom symbiote, but nicer.

This is at least 50% of my current lack of vote (due to uncertainty re: what I think), which I may have to rethink because it might be an unfair comparison >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. It is simple, but well written and fits the historical event in nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main gripe is that I'm surprised they just up and destroyed it. Well, that and I imagined the black thing being the Venom symbiote, but nicer. There needs to be some more to this, I think, beyond "It's a satellite that moves and thinks".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jolly good show.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're like nuclear cyborg Tribbles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually kinda like it. It's simple; leaves many unanswered questions, and is an SCP entity that they couldn't contain, but did successfully deal with.

I don't know the historical context well enough to comment on it, but without that it seems fine to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually don't mind SCPs that played a role in historical events quite as much as others around, as long as their inclusions aren't too ridiculous, and this is certainly well-written. However, I'm a dumb buttface who isn't familiar with the referenced events, and without the tie-in this is just exploring territory that SCPs 1050 and 1947 have already tread in more interesting ways. My apologies, but I'm downvoting. It's not really actively offensive to my tastes, but I'm just not feeling it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addenda seriously overhauled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What this guy said. You neutralize the thing right as the story gets off the ground.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I AM PERFECT. I AM NOMAD. YOU ARE THE CREATOR. YOU ARE THE KIRK.

Nomad aside, I didn't see much meat to this. No mysteries or questions raised. Just a technoorganic satellite and the Foundation using a historical event to take it out. It doesn't look much like Keter material either. Eating a couple satellites and upgrading itself with 60's tech? Is it going to Pong us to death?

What if it started fucking with the space program and endangering human lives? Or what if it kept replicating itself, necessitating an EMP blast to knock them out? Communication logs would be nice too, maybe see what kind of intelligence was forming up there. Give us some hooks, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted for now. As is, the article is extremely abrupt and doesn't give much to explain itself. What was the object that impacted it? Surely it would at least have some research into it. Why would the Foundation simply look at it, think "it's pulsating so it must be semiorganic, fuck it, nuke that thing?'

I think the core idea is sound, but it just needs more of a foundation to stand on. As is, it just jumps out, the article makes a few assumptions and then it's dead. What if it was broadcasting signals? What if it was infecting other satellites, and other instances remained in orbit to this day? What if the Starfish Prime nuke only disabled the electronic portions, but it self-repaired and is still up there, observing with no known motive?

It really needs a hook, otherwise it's just a dead, burnt lump in space. A wise man once said, regular lumps aren't scary, unless you're finding them on your balls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite. This is a no-vote for me, for that reason. It's well written, but just lacks a certain something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: I told you it needsmoar, so it MADE moar!
'boated on account of provided moar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, as much as i like the notes' info, I have to downvote.
I commented on this on IRC, specifically that there's nothing especially interesting about the object at the moment. I realised it's even worse than that. It's one of those objects riding on an interesting historic event, without being nowhere near as interesting as what took place. Sure, it isn't the indonesian tsunami, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A new SCP inspired yesterday by the 50th anniversary of the Starfish Prime experiment, a high-altitude, megaton-yield nuclear test that taught the US (and the world) a harsh lesson about the harmful effects of electromagnetic pulses.

A couple of historical notes:

  • In 1959 the US had begun to develop missiles intended to take out satellites, but they were inaccurate and pretty much useless unless nuclear-tipped anyways. It wasn't until 1985 that a successful take-down of an orbital target was performed by an anti-satellite missile with a conventional warhead.
  • The Fishbowl series of high-altitude nuclear tests were all sub-megaton yield with the exception of Starfish Prime. After the damage it dealt (thousands of lights blown out in Hawaii, one-third of low Earth satellites destroyed), the US used far lower yields in subsequent tests.
  • "The Starfish EMP waveform measured by Richard L. Wakefield of Los Alamos led to a revolution in understanding this nuclear effect."
